aliases: Salmon with Pesto Cream Sauce Pasta

english: Salmon with Pesto Cream Sauce Pasta

food_subgroup: Pasta dish with fish — pan-fried salmon fillet served over spaghetti with a creamy basil pesto sauce containing nuts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for salmon pesto pasta specifically; FOODON_00002572 (pasta dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta dishes — mixed pasta with fish and sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers pasta dishes with fish components; used as closest parent as no specific salmon pesto pasta code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific prepared pasta dish. SNOMED CT does not provide granular codes for restaurant-style composite pasta dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of creamy salmon pesto pasta is approximately 380 g, comprising a pan-fried salmon fillet (approximately 120–140 g) over a bed of spaghetti (approximately 180 g cooked) dressed with a creamy homemade pesto sauce containing nuts.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Cooked spaghetti (FDC ID 168936), Atlantic salmon cooked dry heat (FDC ID 175168), Pesto sauce commercial (FDC ID 172949), and heavy cream (FDC ID 170859); weighted composite calculation for a typical creamy salmon pesto pasta dish.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for creamy salmon pesto pasta exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the primary components: cooked spaghetti (~47% by weight), pan-fried Atlantic salmon (~35% by weight), pesto sauce (~12% by weight), and heavy cream (~6% by weight). Proportions are based on typical restaurant-style plating. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; actual restaurant preparation may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; spaghetti (cooked al dente) GI reference values used as primary carbohydrate driver; cream and salmon contribute negligible glycemic load.
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for creamy salmon pesto pasta as a composite dish. The GI of 45 is estimated using spaghetti (cooked) as the primary glycemic carbohydrate driver, which has a published GI of approximately 45–49 (low GI) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The cream, pesto, and salmon components contribute fat and protein that further attenuate glycemic response, supporting a composite GI estimate at the lower end of the spaghetti reference range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 59.3) / 100 ≈ 26.7 g (carbohydrate per serving = 15.6 g/100g × 380 g serving = 59.3 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1178 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a single serving of creamy salmon pesto pasta provides approximately 1178 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from pesto sauce, parmesan cheese, and seasoning; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~26.7 per serving) driven by spaghetti carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should note that a standard serving of creamy salmon pesto pasta carries a moderate glycemic load of approximately 26.7, driven by the spaghetti base; the fat and protein from salmon and cream attenuate the glycemic response, but portion size should be managed and al dente cooking preferred to minimise postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from salmon offset moderate saturated fat from cream and cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Creamy salmon pesto pasta provides a meaningful source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from the pan-fried salmon fillet, which are cardioprotective; however, the cream and parmesan contribute saturated fat (~11 g per serving), so individuals with cardiovascular disease should consider this dish an occasional rather than daily choice and request lighter cream portions where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Creamy Salmon Pesto Pasta is a Western-style café dish popular in Singapore, consisting of a pan-fried Atlantic salmon fillet served over spaghetti tossed in a homemade creamy basil pesto sauce that contains nuts (typically pine nuts or mixed nuts). The salmon is seared to achieve a golden crust while remaining moist and flaky inside, contributing a rich umami flavour and a significant source of omega-3 fatty acids, high-quality protein, vitamin B12, vitamin D, and selenium. The pesto sauce is made from fresh basil, garlic, parmesan cheese, olive oil, and nuts, blended with cream to produce a velvety, herbaceous coating for the pasta. The dish has a savoury, slightly nutty, and richly creamy flavour profile with a tender pasta texture. It is a composite dish with moderate glycemic load due to the spaghetti base, attenuated by the fat and protein from salmon and cream. Clinically, it is a moderate-sodium dish and should be consumed with portion awareness by individuals managing hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes. The dish contains major allergens including fish, gluten (wheat pasta), tree nuts, and dairy. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal certification without explicit restaurant confirmation. This dish is served at Western-style cafés in Singapore such as The Tree Cafe at Funan mall, priced at approximately SGD 17.90 per plate.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for creamy salmon pesto pasta exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked spaghetti, pan-fried Atlantic salmon, commercial pesto sauce, and heavy cream. The GI is estimated from published spaghetti GI values as the primary carbohydrate driver. Confidence is medium because the component-level USDA data is from approved primary sources, but the composite weighting introduces estimation uncertainty.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; actual values will vary by restaurant preparation, cream quantity, and pesto recipe. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central data for the component ingredients; no industrial trans fats are expected in this dish. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated at 3.1 g from olive oil in pesto and salmon fat profile). 4. The dish contains a nut allergen warning as stated in the original source description ('contains nuts!'). 5. Halal status is unconfirmed — The Tree Cafe at Funan is not listed as a MUIS-certified halal establishment; is_halal is set to false. 6. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate using cooked spaghetti as the primary glycemic carbohydrate driver per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific composite dish. 8. Cuisine tag in input was listed as 'Coffee' (café-style restaurant category on GrabFood); canonical cuisine is corrected to 'Western' based on dish composition. 9. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated values from USDA component data where feasible; no -1 values remain in this record.
